channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Romance Novels Filled With Dazzling Fireworks
item.description: Our columnist on three of the year’s most anticipated books.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Waite

item.title: Book Review: ‘I Want You to Be Happy,’ by Jem Calder
item.description: Jem Calder’s novel, “I Want You to Be Happy,” follows the flailing situationship between two aspiring writers with a 12-year age gap.
item.dc:creator: Lizzy Harding
item.category: I Want You to Be Happy (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Journey to Nowhere,’ by Shiva Naipaul
item.description: In “Journey to Nowhere,” Shiva Naipaul reports on the aftermath of a cult’s disastrous turn away from idealism.
item.dc:creator: Anderson Tepper
item.category: Journey to Nowhere: A New World Tragedy (Book)
People's Temple of Disciples of Christ
item.media:credit: Stuart William MacGladrie/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Shiva Naipaul, while lesser known than his older brother, V.S., published a small but vital body of work.

item.title: Book Review: ‘They Stole a City,’ by Lauren Collins
item.description: A new book by the journalist Lauren Collins chronicles the ongoing effects of the violent takeover of Wilmington, N.C., on those living in its aftermath.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: They Stole a City: Wilmington's White Supremacist Coup and the Families Who Live With Its Legacy (Book)

item.title: Golden Age Mysteries: A Starter Pack
item.description: Interested in these classic novels, but not sure where to start? Let our expert guide you.
item.dc:creator: Sadie Stein

item.title: 75 Years Later, ‘The Catcher in the Rye’ Remains a Great American Novel
item.description: Sure, “Catcher in the Rye” has its detractors. But the influence of its wised-up yet winningly innocent hero keeps it among the Great American Novels.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: J.D. Salinger’s novel has sold some 80 million copies globally. The original cover features an illustration of a carousel horse.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Catch the Devil,’ by Pamela Colloff
item.description: In “Catch the Devil,” Pamela Colloff vividly tells the true story of the con man who traded on other prisoners’ “confessions” for clemency.
item.dc:creator: Ted Conover
item.category: Catch the Devil: A True Story of Murder, Deception, and Injustice on the Gulf Coast (Book)

item.title: What to Read This Summer: NYT Reading Bucket List
item.description: Read along with the Book Review this summer: Can you check off five items before fall arrives? (This year, there are prizes!)
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: Aging Out: An Exploration of Caregiving, Community, and How Americans Grow Old (Book)
A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
Catch the Devil: A True Story of Murder, Deception, and Injustice on the Gulf Coast (Book)
They Stole a City: Wilmington's White Supremacist Coup and the Families Who Live With Its Legacy (Book)
It Will Come Back to You: Collected Stories (Book)

item.title: The Only Thing Riskier Than Telling an Abortion Joke
item.description: For the comedian Alison Leiby, talking about her choice to be child-free is more polarizing to audiences than anything else.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Leiby’s new book, “I’m a Lot,” is a collection of funny, intimate essays that touch on everything from her reality TV addiction to chronic pain.

item.title: Archaeologists Unearth a 2,400-Year-Old Sanctuary for the Worship of Odysseus
item.description: The ruins of a 2,400-year-old complex suggest that, to the Greeks of antiquity, Homer’s protagonist was not just a character in a poem but a figure of genuine devotion.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
item.media:credit: Fine Art Images/Heritage Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A 19th-century painting by the Swiss painter Arnold Bocklin depicting Odysseus fleeing the Cyclops Polyphemus.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Air,’ by Christian Kracht
item.description: In “Air,” by the Swiss writer Christian Kracht, a decorator’s unusual commission leads abruptly into another universe.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Lee

item.title: Book Review: ‘Cloudthief,’ by Nathaniel Rich
item.description: “Cloudthief,” by Nathaniel Rich, follows a dispirited writer into a scheme to get rich and find romance along the way.
item.dc:creator: Peter C. Baker

item.title: Book Review: ‘Famous Men,’ by Julie Buntin
item.description: Julie Buntin’s novel “Famous Men” complicates an old story with questions of ambition and consent.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Christensen

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Gospel According to Hobby Lobby,’ by Michael Blanding
item.description: A new book by Michael Blanding chronicles the history of Hobby Lobby and its billionaire founder’s campaign to promote conservative Christian values.
item.dc:creator: Virginia Heffernan
item.category: The Gospel According to Hobby Lobby: Inside a Billionaire Family's Quest to Craft a Christian Nation (Book)
item.media:description: David Green and his family built Hobby Lobby into a Christian nationalist ministry. “Our goal is just to be a light to the world,” Darsee Lett, David’s daughter and Hobby Lobby’s creative director, has said.

item.title: Shahrnush Parsipur, Iranian Writer Imprisoned for Her Novels, Dies at 80
item.description: Her best-known work, “Women Without Men,” was longlisted for the International Booker Prize this year.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Shahrnush Parsipur in 2009. Her novels and stories about women in Iran before and after the Islamic Revolution made her one of the country’s most acclaimed writers of her generation.

item.title: Book Review: ‘It Will Come Back to You,’ by Sigrid Nunez
item.description: Her first collection of short fiction, “It Will Come Back to You,” is threaded with erudition but never pretentious.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.category: It Will Come Back to You: Collected Stories (Book)
item.media:credit: Clement Pascal for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sigrid Nunez has maintained in her work a mistrust of cultural hypersensitivity and thought policing.

item.title: Sizzling, Sexy Age-Gap Romance Novels
item.description: The best-selling author Rachel Lynn Solomon recommends forbidden love stories full of fiery desire, shifting power dynamics and so-wrong-it’s-right drama.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Lynn Solomon
item.media:credit: The New York Times; Author photo by Sabreen Lakhani

item.title: Book Review: ‘Aging Out,’ by Lucy Schiller
item.description: From assisted living to elder activism and her own experience as a caregiver, Lucy Schiller investigates aging’s myriad challenges in her first book.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Orange
item.category: Aging Out: An Exploration of Caregiving, Community, and How Americans Grow Old (Book)
item.media:credit: Carolyn Fong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Though not yet old herself, Schiller claims intimate knowledge of conditions we associate with old age: invisibility, precarity, isolation.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Every Inch a Lady,’ by Audrey Smaltz
item.description: In “Every Inch a Lady,” Audrey Smaltz shares her incredible story — if not her inimitable voice.
item.dc:creator: Roxanne Fequiere
item.category: Every Inch a Lady: My Amazing Journey From Dear Harlem to Haute Couture & Beyond (Book)
item.media:description: A beauty queen, model, executive, editor and fashion insider, Smaltz is something of a living industry legend.

item.title: Our Insatiable Appetite for Stories About Cannibalism
item.description: Why do the characters in so many new novels insist upon eating each other?
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Book Review: ‘Fixer Chao,’ by Han Ong
item.description: Han Ong’s barbed, irreverent portrait of identity, class envy and flimflam deception in turn-of-the-21st-century Manhattan still resonates.
item.dc:creator: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: Historian Kerri K. Greenidge and Her Book ‘The Grimkes’ Come Under Fire
item.description: Kerri K. Greenidge appeared to lose her professorship at Tufts University after scholars began scrutinizing her 2022 book, “The Grimkes,” which is no longer listed on its publisher’s website.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Black Radical: The Life and Times of William Monroe Trotter (Book)
item.media:credit: Mark Elzey Jr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Historians are questioning the validity of multiple works by the historian Kerri K. Greenidge, who is no longer employed by Tufts University.

item.title: Hershel Parker, Melville Scholar of Ahab-Like Obsessiveness, Dies at 90
item.description: The author of an exhaustive two-volume biography, he was “fanatical about his approach to scholarship and infatuated with Melville,” a colleague said.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Dinaw Mengestu Resigns as PEN America President After Israel Article
item.description: The novelist Dinaw Mengestu, who was elected seven months ago, said the article “continues this approach toward defending some rights while not defending others.”
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:description: Dinaw Mengestu had been on PEN America’s board for about a decade before he became its president in December.

item.title: This Author Says You Shouldn’t Be Intimidated by ‘The Odyssey’
item.description: First, a primer from the author of “Circe,” Madeline Miller. Then, A.O. Scott on all the genres inside the 3,000-year-old poem.
item.dc:creator: Gilbert Cruz, Sarah Diamond, Amy Pearl, Patricia Sulbarán and Tracy Mumford
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Madeline Miller: Christopher Leaman for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Vast Horizon,’ by Anna Thomasson
item.description: A group of artists gathered at a hotel on the Côte d’Azur in 1937. A new book by Anna Thomasson captures the art and escapades the holiday inspired.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin
item.category: A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
item.media:description: Pablo Picasso and Dora Maar at Golfe-Juan, France, on the Côte d’Azur in 1937.

item.title: 10 Kids’ Books for Fans of the Magic Tree House Series
item.description: Ten recommendations for fans of Mary Pope Osborne’s time-travel chapter book series.
item.dc:creator: Travis Jonker
item.category: The Story of Gumluck the Wizard (Book)

item.title: From A.I. to the Deep State, Michel Foucault Foresaw It All
item.description: One hundred years after his birth, the French philosopher remains hugely influential, both revered and reviled for ideas that eerily anticipated our day.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin
